text: The surgeon introduces six cannulas through marked incisions in the abdomen, revealing yellow fat and red muscle tissue. The camera view moves to the right upper part of the body, identifying the grayish structure of the colon. The surgeon uses graspers to measure the small intestines, marking every four inches, and places a clip at the five-foot mark for future intestinal realignment. A stitch is placed to secure this section to the abdominal wall for easy identification during subsequent surgical steps.

The surgeon uses a liver retractor to lift the brownish liver, revealing the pinkish stomach beneath. The stomach is detached from surrounding structures, including the diaphragm, and an endoscope is introduced into the stomach as a guide. A stapler is used to place three rows of titanium staples and simultaneously cut the stomach, creating a sleeve gastrectomy. The separated portion of the stomach is visible on the right side, while the sleeve stomach remains on the left.

The surgeon continues stapling and cutting along the endoscope in the stomach, creating a sleeve gastrectomy. The sleeve stomach is visible on the left side, while the duodenum is on the right. The surgeon detaches the duodenum from surrounding fat and creates a tunnel behind it, near the pancreas. Bleeding is controlled using stitches, clips, or other devices. The duodenum is stapled about an inch and a half below the stomach, with visible blood on the left side, which is stopped with stitches.

The surgeon creates a hole in the duodenum and connects it to the previously marked section of the intestines. Yellow bile fluid is visible as the connection is made. Due to excess fat, some tissue is removed to facilitate the connection. The surgeon uses a stapler to create a wide opening, ensuring no narrowing occurs. Stitches are placed to reinforce the connection and prevent leakage. The gallbladder is visible in the background.

The surgeon performs a duodenal switch by cutting and reconnecting the duodenum to the intestines. The middle section is where pancreatic and bile juices flow, aiding in fat and protein digestion. A leak test is conducted by blowing air into the sleeve stomach and checking for bubbles underwater, ensuring no leaks. The intestines are then connected using a stapler, similar to the gastric bypass technique, ensuring a wide connection to prevent kinking or blockages.

The surgeon closes a small opening in the intestine using a stapler and reinforces the corners with stitches to prevent leaks and tension. The connection allows food to flow from the right bottom intestine to the left bottom intestine and then to the colon, facilitating absorption over the last five feet. The detached stomach is placed in a plastic bag to prevent contamination and is removed through a half-inch incision. The surgeon checks for air leaks and stitches the incisions to prevent hernias, then washes the incisions and removes the cannulas.
